topic: Type System - Marker Struct for Context

turns.content: I thought I could just use a regular struct to hold the database connection details so that my Database trait would be generic over Postgres and Sqlite, but it keeps failing to compile because the type parameter isn't being treated as a marker. Why is this happening?

The issue is that a regular struct with fields makes the compiler treat those fields as concrete data you can inspect or clone, which breaks your ability to swap implementations without changing code. Think of it like trying to use a keycard for access control but making it a physical plastic card; once the ID number on the card changes, you have to replace the whole card. A marker struct is more like an RFID tag that only proves identity without carrying extra data. When you define your Database trait with a generic type parameter T where T implements some marker trait, T acts as a phantom parameter that tells the compiler which specific database implementation you want to use—Postgres or Sqlite—without actually storing any runtime values in it.

Okay so I need to create this ZST wrapper but I'm confused about what exactly makes a type act like a marker versus just another regular data structure. It feels like I'm trying to force the type system to do something that doesn't make sense logically.

The key distinction is whether your generic parameter carries actual values or only influences how other code behaves. If you create a simple enum with two variants for Postgres and Sqlite, it acts as a marker because the compiler uses it purely to select behavior without storing data about those variants at runtime. Imagine building two different versions of a machine where only the control panel changes but the engine remains identical; the control panel is your marker struct while the engine represents the actual database implementation details. Your trait can then use this marker to dispatch specific logic based on which version you chose.

That analogy makes more sense now, but I'm still struggling to wrap my head around how the compiler actually uses this phantom parameter during type checking versus runtime execution since there's literally no data being stored in it.

During compilation, the compiler analyzes your trait methods and sees that T is only used as a marker to select behavior—Postgres or Sqlite logic—but never stores any value from T. At runtime, when you call Database::<Postgres>::new(), the actual Postgres connection object gets created separately through the trait's implementation details, while the T parameter in your trait definition remains empty like an invisible placeholder. This separation allows you to write one generic interface that works with multiple concrete implementations without duplicating code for each database type.

I see how it separates the interface from the implementation but I'm worried this will cause performance issues if someone tries to access T's data later because we're essentially lying about what T represents.

You can't actually access T's data once you've defined your trait correctly, so there's no hidden overhead or performance penalty. The compiler enforces that T is only used as a marker during the compilation phase to ensure type safety and dispatch correctness. If someone tries to use T for storing actual data after defining it this way, the code will simply fail to compile because T has no memory footprint—it's purely a compile-time construct. This design pattern ensures your trait remains efficient regardless of how many different database implementations you support.
Alright I think I understand now but can you show me how to actually implement this with my Database trait so I can stop guessing about what goes where?

You'll define your Database trait with a generic type parameter T, then add an associated type or method that accepts T as its marker. Inside the trait's implementation blocks for Postgres and Sqlite, you use T only to determine which specific database logic applies—no fields are ever stored in instances of T itself. For example, when defining your Database<Postgres> instance, the actual connection pool goes into a separate field while T remains an empty marker that simply tells the compiler to execute the Postgres-specific code path.

Wait I just realized I might have been using serde_json::Value or some other serialization type as my marker instead of a proper enum which is causing all this confusion. How do I ensure my marker struct actually works correctly?

The simplest approach is to define your own enum with variants named Postgres and Sqlite, then use that enum as your generic parameter T in the trait definition. This ensures the compiler recognizes it purely as a marker without any unexpected serialization or data storage behavior interfering with your type system logic. When you implement the trait for these specific types, the compiler will correctly dispatch based on which variant is chosen while keeping the actual database connection details separate from the marker itself.
